text.headline: Apple Computer Founded
text.text: Steve Jobs, Steve Wozniak, and Ronald Wayne establish Apple in a garage. While Wayne sold his share for $800 just days later, the duo of 'Woz' and Jobs would go on to ignite the personal computer revolution.

media.caption: Wozniak, Jobs, and the early days of Apple.

text.headline: Apple II: The First 'Personal' Computer
text.text: The Apple II becomes a massive success, featuring color graphics and an open architecture. It was the machine that turned Apple into a real company and dominated the 8-bit era.

media.caption: The Apple II: Color graphics for the masses.

text.headline: Macintosh: 'For the Rest of Us'
text.text: Introduced with a dystopian Super Bowl ad, the Macintosh was the first successful mass-market computer with a Graphical User Interface (GUI) and a mouse. It aimed to make computing intuitive rather than intimidating.

media.caption: Steve Jobs and the original Macintosh 128K.

text.headline: The Ousting of Steve Jobs
text.text: Following a power struggle with CEO John Sculley, Steve Jobs resigns from Apple. He would soon found NeXT Inc., developing the advanced 'NeXTSTEP' OS that would eventually save Apple 12 years later.

media.caption: The 'wilderness years' for Jobs began with his departure in 1985.

text.headline: Apple Acquires NeXT
text.text: In a desperate move to replace the aging 'Classic' Mac OS, Apple buys NeXT for $400 million. This brings Steve Jobs back as an advisor and provides the Unix-based foundation for what would become macOS.

media.caption: The merger that saved Apple and redefined its software DNA.

text.headline: iMac G3: The 'Bondi Blue' Comeback
text.text: The first major product after Jobs' return, the translucent iMac G3 abandoned the floppy drive for USB and focused on the internet. It was a massive hit that restored Apple's profitability.

media.caption: The iMac G3 made computers 'fun' again.

text.headline: Mac OS X: The Aqua Era
text.text: Apple launches Mac OS X 10.0 'Cheetah.' It merged the user-friendly Mac interface with a rock-solid Unix core, finally providing the stability and multitasking power users had craved for years.

media.caption: The 'Aqua' interface of Mac OS X Cheetah.

text.headline: iPod: '1,000 Songs in Your Pocket'
text.text: Announced with Jobs' simple promise of '1,000 songs in your pocket,' the iPod was dismissed by critics as overpriced at $399. They were wrong. Within three years it controlled 70% of the digital music market and transformed Apple from a struggling computer maker into a consumer electronics powerhouse. By tying the iPod to the iTunes Store in 2003, Apple reinvented the music industry and built the ecosystem model that would later define the iPhone — proving that hardware and software combined in one seamless experience was worth any price.

media.caption: The original first-generation iPod, released November 10, 2001.

text.headline: The Intel Transition
text.text: Steve Jobs announces that Apple will abandon PowerPC processors in favor of Intel. This allowed Macs to run much faster and cooler, and even enabled them to run Windows natively via Boot Camp.

media.caption: The 'impossible' switch to Intel chips in 2006.

text.headline: iPhone: 'OS X' in Your Pocket
text.text: The original iPhone redefined mobile computing. Jobs famously stated it ran 'OS X,' highlighting that the desktop and mobile operating systems shared the same powerful foundation.

media.caption: Three devices in one: a phone, an iPod, and an internet communicator.

text.headline: The App Store: Inventing the App Economy
text.text: Launched with 500 apps and a 70/30 revenue split in developers' favor, the App Store created an entirely new category of business: the mobile app. Jobs had initially resisted it, preferring web apps for the iPhone — he was persuaded by Phil Schiller and board member Art Levinson. The decision proved transformative: within a decade, the App Store had paid out over $100 billion to developers, created millions of jobs, and spawned entirely new industries. It also gave Apple unprecedented control over software distribution that regulators are still reckoning with today.

media.caption: The App Store icon — the gateway to a software economy Apple never originally planned.

text.headline: iPad: A New Category of Device
text.text: Unveiled at the Yerba Buena Center in San Francisco, the iPad arrived to nearly universal skepticism — critics called it 'just a big iPod touch.' Jobs countered that it required its own category: 'Post-PC,' in which the most important things people do each day don't require a traditional computer. He was right. The iPad's 9.7-inch touchscreen became the dominant form for reading, media consumption, and casual work, and spawned an entire tablet industry that simply did not exist before January 27, 2010.

media.caption: Steve Jobs unveils the original iPad at Apple's January 2010 event.

text.headline: iCloud: The Post-PC Vision
text.text: Steve Jobs' final major product introduction. iCloud moved the center of the user's digital life from the Mac to the Cloud, ensuring data parity across all Apple devices automatically.

text.headline: The End of an Era: Steve Jobs, 1955–2011
text.text: Steve Jobs passed away on October 5, 2011, at 56 — one day after Apple unveiled the iPhone 4S. Within hours, memorials of apples, flowers, and handwritten notes blanketed Apple Stores from Cupertino to Tokyo. Under Jobs, Apple had returned from near-bankruptcy to become the most valuable company in the world, launching the iMac, iPod, iTunes, iPhone, iPad, and App Store in a thirteen-year run unmatched in tech history. His successor, Tim Cook, would guide Apple past $3 trillion in market capitalization, but the question of who Apple is without Jobs still shapes every product decision made in Cupertino.

media.caption: A makeshift memorial at Stanford University in Palo Alto, October 2011.

text.headline: Apple Silicon: Total Control
text.text: Apple begins the transition from Intel to its custom-designed M1 chips. By controlling the hardware and the software, Apple achieved a massive leap in performance-per-watt, reunifying the Mac and iPhone architectures.

media.caption: The M1 chip: A new era for the Mac.

text.headline: Vision Pro: Computing Without a Screen
text.text: Announced at WWDC 2023 and released on February 2, 2024, at $3,499, the Apple Vision Pro is Apple's most ambitious hardware bet since the original iPhone. The headset introduces 'spatial computing' — merging digital windows, apps, and media directly into the user's physical environment using ultra-high-density micro-OLED displays and a novel eye-tracking input system. Internally, Apple considers it the successor to both the Mac and the iPhone. Whether Vision Pro becomes the defining computing form factor of the next decade or an expensive footnote will define Tim Cook's legacy.

media.caption: A user experiencing Apple Vision Pro's spatial computing environment.
media.credit: Ramsey Cardy / Collision via Sportsfile (CC BY 2.0)
